text: In the elective full thickness rectal prolapse surgery, the patient is positioned in jackknife, and a Lone Star retractor is used to expose the surgical field. Babcock forceps are employed to avert the rectal prolapse, allowing identification of the dentin line. The prolapse is measured, and an incision is made two centimeters above the dentin line. Dissection of the rectum and mesorectum proceeds through a vascular plane, with small mesorectal vessels being coagulated and interrupted near the rectal wall.

In the emergency surgery, the surgeon encounters difficulty identifying the dentin line due to venous congestion and tissue edema. Bleeding occurs during the rectal section, prompting the use of single stitches and an ultrasound device for tissue management instead of an electroscalpel.

The surgeon reaches the sigmoid colon and completes the circumferential section of the mesorectum. In the emergency scenario, the sigmoid colon is lifted using a plastic loop to facilitate the maneuver.

During the elective surgery, the surgeon performs a levatorplasty, addressing the diastasis of the levator ani muscle branches. A linear stapler is used to divide the sigmoid colon, followed by a stitch-by-stitch coloanal anastomosis. The retractor is then removed.
In the emergency case, the surgeon performs a laparoscopic diverting colostomy, visualizing the altered tissue involved in the anastomosis to mitigate the high risk of leakage.
